title: Falls Prevention in Home Health: What Operators Need to Know in 2026
description: Falls prevention in home health is changing. HHVBP, objective screening and standardisation are reshaping how operators protect outcomes and revenue in 2026.
author: Able Care Clinical Team
excerpt: Falls remain the leading cause of injury-related hospitalisation for older adults receiving home health services. In 2026, operators face new pressures from HHVBP expansion, payer scrutiny and staffing constraints. Here is what has changed and what operators can do about it.
content:
The scale of the problem

Falls are the leading cause of injury-related hospitalisation for adults over 65 in the United States. For home health agencies, every fall that leads to a hospitalisation impacts HHVBP performance, increases costs and creates a cascade of clinical and operational consequences.

The challenge is that most home health agencies still rely on subjective, paper-based falls assessments. These take 20+ minutes, vary from clinician to clinician and rarely feed into a structured care plan. The result is inconsistent screening, missed decline and preventable hospitalisations.

What has changed in 2026

Three forces are reshaping falls prevention in home health.

HHVBP expansion. The Home Health Value-Based Purchasing model now covers every Medicare-certified home health agency in the US. Falls outcomes feed HHVBP measures directly. Agencies that reduce hospitalisations from falls protect their HHVBP performance and revenue. Agencies that do not face financial penalties.

Objective screening technology. Digital, sensor-based screening tools like Able Assess now make it possible to capture objective, standardised falls risk data in under five minutes, using non-clinical staff. This eliminates the variability of paper-based assessments and creates a data trail that is defensible to payers and regulators.

Staffing constraints. The home health workforce is under sustained pressure. Clinical staff are expensive and hard to retain. Any tool that enables non-clinical staff to deliver clinical-quality screening frees clinicians to focus on care planning and complex cases.

What operators can do

Standardise screening across every branch. The single biggest lever for multi-site operators is moving from inconsistent, clinician-dependent paper assessments to a single, standardised protocol that any staff member can run. This is particularly important for PE-backed and franchised operators who need defensible, consistent outcomes data across every branch.

Screen objectively. Objective screening using validated metrics (grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed, Timed Up and Go) is more predictive than subjective observation. The evidence shows that multifactorial, objective screening reduces falls by up to 25%.

Act on the data. Screening only matters if it triggers action. The best tools route risk banding directly to the care team, triggering care plan updates, referrals and follow-up assessments automatically.

Track outcomes over time. Longitudinal trending surfaces decline before it becomes a fall. This is the difference between reactive care and proactive prevention.

The HHVBP connection

Falls outcomes feed HHVBP measures directly. Every prevented hospitalisation from a fall improves your HHVBP score. For a 12-branch operator, preventing one fall-related hospitalisation per branch per month can represent significant HHVBP upside. The ROI calculator on our resources page models this for your specific scenario.

Key takeaways

  • Falls remain the leading cause of injury-related hospitalisation for older adults in home health.
  • HHVBP expansion means falls outcomes now directly impact revenue for every Medicare-certified agency.
  • Objective, standardised screening reduces falls by up to 25% and creates defensible data for payers.
  • Non-clinical staff can deliver clinical-quality screening in under five minutes with the right technology.
  • Longitudinal tracking surfaces decline before it becomes a fall or a hospitalisation.
